title.emphasis: A Deduced Spectrum of Cooperative Securities

section.para: This tension between patronage and non-patronage income allows us to deduce the "implicit spectra of securities" you described. These instruments can be categorized by the nature of the income stream that backs them, creating a range from purely cooperative to purely commercial.
These instruments are at the most "cooperative" end of the spectrum. Their value is intrinsically linked to member participation, not capital appreciation.

listitem.para: Membership Shares & Allocated Equity: These include initial membership shares, retained patronage refunds, and per-unit capital retains.8 They are typically non-transferable, have no dividend rights separate from patronage, and are redeemed at par value. Their primary purpose is to represent a member's right to use and benefit from the cooperative. The SEC has often agreed that these instruments do not constitute "securities" under federal law precisely because they lack the characteristics of an investment.

section.para: At the opposite end of the spectrum are the publicly-traded Class B investor shares envisioned in the hybrid cooperative model.

listitem.para: Class B Investor Shares: These are designed to be nearly indistinguishable from traditional corporate stock. Their value is driven by market sentiment and the cooperative's overall profitability (including non-patronage income). They confer voting rights on a per-share basis and pay profit-based dividends, not patronage.10 These are unambiguously "securities" that would trade on major exchanges.

section.para: 3. Securitized Cooperative Income Streams (The Hybrid Middle)
This is where the framework becomes truly innovative, allowing for the creation of new asset classes by securitizing the distinct income streams of a cooperative or a pool of cooperatives.11

listitem.para: Patronage-Backed Security (PBS): This would be a financial instrument backed exclusively by the future flow of patronage dividends from a cooperative. The return on a PBS would be directly tied to the volume of business members conduct with the cooperative. Its appeal would be to investors seeking stable, predictable returns correlated with real economic activity rather than market speculation.9 This is the purest form of a tradable "cooperative" security.

Non-Patronage-Backed Security (NPBS): This instrument would be backed by the income generated from a cooperative's non-patronage activities—such as returns from a portfolio of passive investments or profits from business with non-members. An NPBS would behave much more like a traditional corporate bond or preferred stock, with returns dependent on the success of the cooperative's commercial side-ventures.4

Blended-Income Security (BIS): A third possibility is a security backed by the total net income of the cooperative, combining both patronage and non-patronage streams. The valuation of a BIS would be a complex but fascinating blend of the stability of the member-patron base and the growth potential of the cooperative's external investments.

section.para: This spectrum provides a robust conceptual framework. It demonstrates how the foundational tax rules of cooperative enterprise can be used to engineer a variety of new financial instruments, each with a unique risk/return profile, that could be tailored for different types of investors and traded on different markets.
